chunks.text: T. Kenneth Gustafson is Professor Emeritus at UC Berkeley's EECS department. He earned his BASc and MASc in Electrical Engineering from the University of British Columbia and a Ph.D. from MIT in 1968. His MIT thesis focused on nonlinear optical light pulse propagation, including self-steepening, self-phase modulation, and thermodynamic implications. While at MIT, he consulted for Lincoln Laboratory.

After graduation, Professor Gustafson joined UC Berkeley's EECS, continuing work on nonlinear optical pulse propagation. He suggested external self-phase modulation for optical pulse compression. Other work included coherent pulse probe measurements of rotational Raman structure in molecular gases and using tunneling structures for coherent optical detection. He is a fellow of the Optical Society of America and a member of the American Physical Society and IEEE.

His recent interests included applications of coherent electron-hole interactions in semiconductors and quantum fluctuations in optical devices and communications systems. Professor Gustafson passed away in October 2025.

Selected publications include: 'Orthogonal photorefractive grating storage' in Optics Letters (1996), 'Photoinduced currents in metal-barrier-metal junctions' in a NASA conference proceedings (1978), 'Diagrammatic analysis of the density operator for nonlinear optical calculations' in Physical Review A (1978), and 'Stimulated emission of surface plasmons by electron tunneling' in Applied Physics Letters (1978).

Additional publications: 'Characteristics of integrated MOM junctions at dc and at optical frequencies' in IEEE J. Quantum Electronics (1978), 'An integrated metal-oxide-metal device' in IEEE J. Quantum Electronics (1977), 'Edge-MOM diode: An integrated, optical, nonlinear device' in IEEE Trans. Electron Devices (1977), and 'Ultrabroadband integrated non-linear device' in an OSA conference (1977).

Further publications: 'Coupling characteristics of thin-film metal-oxide-metal diodes at 10.6 mu m' in Applied Physics Letters (1975), 'Observation of coherent transient birefringence in CS2 vapor' in Physical Review Letters (1975), 'Self-phase modulation of picosecond pulses in optical fibers' in Applied Physics Letters (1974), and 'Detection of optical and infrared radiation with DC-biased electron-tunneling metal-barrier-metal diodes' in IEEE J. Quantum Electronics (1973).

Earlier publications: 'Thermally self-induced phase modulation of laser beams' in Applied Physics Letters (1970) and 'Subpicosecond pulse generation using the optical Kerr effect' in Applied Physics Letters (1969). Selected honors include IEEE Fellow (1991) and Optica Fellow (formerly OSA Fellow, 1980).
